coredata.dc:title: Development of FRET-based optical sensors using N-doped carbon dots for detection of chromium (VI) and manganese (VII) in water for a sustainable future

coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs) synthesis has received much attention due to their considerable application potential. Natural compounds are efficient CD carbon precursors, exhibiting significant physical and chemical characteristics. In the present work, the hydrothermal method has successfully derived blue fluorescent N-doped carbon dots (NCDs) from Alstonia scholaris leaves and ethylenediamine. The synthesized NCDs were characterized by Fourier transform infrared (FTIR), Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM), fluorescence and UV–vis analysis. It exhibits better chemical and physical properties with a relatively good quantum yield of 21%. As-synthesized NCDs were applied for the FRET-based detection of Cr6+ and Mn7+ ions in water samples to investigate their suitability as fluorescent probes. The detection limit was 0.173 µM and 0.394 µM for Cr6+ and Mn7+, respectively. This study has illustrated an environmentally friendly approach for the facile synthesis of blue fluorescent NCDs with remarkable potential for further utilization in sensing devices.

head.ce:title: Development of FRET-based optical sensors using N-doped carbon dots for detection of chromium (VI) and manganese (VII) in water for a sustainable future

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs) synthesis has received much attention due to their considerable application potential. Natural compounds are efficient CD carbon precursors, exhibiting significant physical and chemical characteristics. In the present work, the hydrothermal method has successfully derived blue fluorescent N-doped carbon dots (NCDs) from Alstonia scholaris leaves and ethylenediamine. The synthesized NCDs were characterized by Fourier transform infrared (FTIR), Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM), fluorescence and UV–vis analysis. It exhibits better chemical and physical properties with a relatively good quantum yield of 21%. As-synthesized NCDs were applied for the FRET-based detection of Cr6+ and Mn7+ ions in water samples to investigate their suitability as fluorescent probes. The detection limit was 0.173 µM and 0.394 µM for Cr6+ and Mn7+, respectively. This study has illustrated an environmentally friendly approach for the facile synthesis of blue fluorescent NCDs with remarkable potential for further utilization in sensing devices.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Biomass-derived carbon dots have emerged as a ground-breaking innovation in environmental monitoring and water quality assessment. The carbon-based nanoparticles derived from natural sources offer promising solutions to address the challenges of different harmful contaminants [8]. Among all the toxic contaminants, hexavalent chromium (Cr6+) and manganese ions (Mn7+) are commonly found in industrial wastewater and agricultural runoff [10,74]. Hexavalent chromium (Cr6+) finds extensive application in various industrial processes, like leather tanning, electroplating, and metallurgy [11,32,48]. Excessive consumption of chromate can adversely affect the proteins, DNA, and enzyme systems in human bodies, resulting in significant cytotoxicity and potential carcinogenicity [4,38,42,58,71].

Conversely, permanganate (MnO4 -) is widely employed as a potent oxidant in laboratory and industrial applications. Excessive exposure to permanganate may adversely affect human health, causing deformities and respiratory damage. Thus, the US Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) has designated chromate and permanganate as top-priority pollutants [11,17,65]. Therefore, there is a need to develop reliable, sensitive, and cost-effective detection techniques to monitor their concentrations in aqueous media and mitigate their detrimental effects.

Until now, various analytical methods have been reported for sensing Cr6+ and Mn7+ ions, including high-performance liquid chromatography [12,14], electrochemical sensors [50], surface plasmon resonance [29], ﬂuorescence spectrophotometry [9,44] and UV–vis absorbance spectroscopy (UV–vis) [8]. Among all the established techniques, optical techniques have gained significant interest due to advantages such as high sensitivity, cost-effectiveness, practical applicability, rapid response, and simplicity. Various reports of fluorescent-based sensors using different materials, including organic polymers, metal-organic frameworks, and heavy metal sulfide quantum dots, have been developed. The synthetic procedure of many materials involves harmful chemicals and time-consuming preparation methods that limit their practical applicability [39,46]. Therefore, there is a need to develop on-spot detection techniques that are rapid, cost-effective, and sensitive sensor systems composed of environmentally friendly components. Such a system would address the current limitations and meet the requirements of modern applications.

To enhance the detection of Cr6+ and Mn7+ ions, numerous luminescent sensors encompassing carbon dots (CDs) and metal-organic coordination polymers have been developed [20,67]. Among all the materials, CDs have been widely used as electrochemical and fluorescent sensors [30,59]. The emergence of fluorescent CDs significantly impacts various fields, including medicine, electronics, energy photonics, catalysis, and sensing. The ease of fabrication, photostability, biocompatibility, and low toxicity of CDs have generated considerable interest [23]. Considerable growth has been observed for fluorescent probes to detect different pollutants [55,68]. Among all the present mechanistic approaches for fluorescent-based sensors, the evolution of Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET)- based optical sensors has not received much attention. FRET is based on an energy transfer mechanism that excites the fluorescent probe molecules [40]. Unfortunately, there have been limited reports on FRET-based systems that detect toxic metal ions. This scarcity of research highlights the significant interest and can be considered an exceptional approach for identifying toxic metal ions.

Many procedures have been used to synthesize CDs using carbon-based material as a source. However, there is a need to develop some novel and green approaches for the synthesis of CDs. Green synthesis of CDs involves using natural precursors or chemicals derived from renewable natural products or processes as the carbon source [52]. Incorporating heteroatoms into the carbon dots framework is a well-known strategy to enhance the properties of carbon dots, including their electrical, optical, and other characteristics, leading to more promising applications [54].

Alstonia scholaris (AS), widely known as saptparni, is a medicinal plant in various parts of Asia. These leaves are primarily used in traditional medicine due to their antibacterial, antiviral, anti-inflammatory, and anticancer characteristics [31]. AS has a rich history in herbal medicine and possesses numerous well-documented pharmacological characteristics [47]. Previous studies have explored the utilization of AS for the production of Fe2MgO4 [69], ZnO [56], Ag [51], and various nanoparticles. However, there is a lack of research on carbon dots derived from this plant.

Herein, for the first time,
•
We have synthesized highly luminescent N-doped CDs (NCDs) utilizing Alstonia scholaris leaves and ethylenediamine.
•
We have utilized this highly fluorescent material to develop a FRET-based sensing platform for detecting Cr6+ and Mn7+ ions.
•
Furthermore, we have also used the material as a filter paper-based sensing platform for visual detection of Cr6+ and Mn7+ ions under UV light with a wavelength of 365 nm.
We anticipate that the developed optical sensor will draw the scientific community's interest due to its remarkable features.

Fresh Alstonia scholaris leaves were collected from the campus of Gauhati University (Jalukbari, Guwahati, India). Ethylenediamine and the metal salts were obtained from Merck Ltd., India, and utilized without additional processing. The solvent employed in the experiment was deionized water, also known as distilled water. The Cr6+ and Mn7+ solutions were prepared using K2Cr2O7 and KMnO4, respectively. The metal cation solutions were prepared using their respective nitrate, sulfate, or chloride salts, whereas the anionic solutions were prepared using sodium, ammonium, and potassium salts.

A hydrothermal carbonization technique was opted to synthesize the fluorescent NCDs from Alstonia scholaris (AS) leaves as a carbon source. Before synthesizing, the collected AS leaves were rinsed thoroughly with distilled water, cut into small pieces, and then air-dried at ambient conditions. The desiccated leaves were pulverized into a fine powder utilizing a household grinding apparatus. A quantity of 0.2 g of leaf powder was placed into a beaker containing 20 milliliters of distilled water. The mixture was then stirred at 60 degrees Celsius for 30 min.

Following filtration, a solution containing ethylenediamine at 0.1 M, 0.05 M, and 0.025 M was gradually introduced into the system while maintaining a stirring condition. The mixture was introduced into an autoclave reactor and subsequently subjected to a temperature of 180 °C for 10 h. Subsequently, the substance was allowed to reach ambient temperature through the cooling process. The solution was centrifuged at a speed of 3000 revolutions per minute for 30 min to eliminate macroscopic particles. The resulting outcome of this procedure produces a supernatant liquid with a distinct yellowish-brown hue, passed through a syringe filter with a pore size of 0.22 µm. The filtered liquid is then stored at a temperature of 5 °C for subsequent analysis and evaluation. Observing a vibrant blue fluorescence emitted by the solution upon exposure to ultraviolet radiation indicates the successful synthesis of carbon dots (CDs).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of hexavalent chromium (Cr6+) and manganese ion (Mn7+)
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence behavior of NCDs was investigated by obtaining their ﬂuorescence spectra at an excitation wavelength of 370 nm that displayed an emission maximum of 456 nm. To explore the excitation-dependent characteristics of the system, we recorded fluorescence spectra across a wavelength range from 300 to 400 nm. These measurements yielded distinct emission maxima with different intensity levels. This observation elucidates the excitation-dependent emission behavior of NCDs. The experimental procedure for investigating the quenching phenomena of NCDs in the presence of chromium (Cr6+) and manganese (Mn7+) ions entails the introduction of a 2.5 mL aliquot of the diluted NCD solution into a quartz cuvette. Subsequently, fluorescence spectra are acquired using an excitation wavelength of 370 nm. After that, a precise amount of the analyte solution was added using a micropipette, appropriately mixed, and the resultant ﬂuorescence spectra were recorded. This method was repeated stepwise, with slightly increasing concentrations of the analyte solution, and the ﬂuorescence spectra were consistently measured using the same excitation wavelength. For the selectivity experiments, 100 µL of a 1 mM solution containing various foreign substances, such as metal cations (Ag+, Zn2+, Cd2+, Ca2+, Pb2+, Co2+, Hg2+, Cu2+, Fe3+, Ni2+) and anions (Cl–, NO3 –, F–, I–, CO3 2–, HPO4 –, HPO4 2–, S2–), was employed for evaluation.

In the first step, Whatman filter paper of grade 1 was divided into rectangular sections measuring 1 cm × 4 cm. These sections were then placed in a solution of NCDs and left to soak for approximately 5 min. Subsequently, NCDs were infused into filter paper fragments and subjected to a 30-minute drying in a hot air oven. In the context of visual detection, the filter paper samples were subjected to varying concentrations of Cr6+ and Mn7+ in an aqueous solution. The digital images were acquired using a UV illumination chamber with a wavelength of 365 nm.

The antibacterial activity of the test samples was determined using the Kirby-Bauer Disc Diffusion Susceptibility Test Protocol on Pseudomonas sp. and Bacillus sp. Hudzicki ($year$) [26]. The bacteria were initially cultivated on nutrient agar plates before being put in an incubator set to 37 °C for 24 h to allow the cultures to reach their log phase to conduct the tests [7]. The test samples were produced by dissolving them in suitable solvents to generate stock solutions at the relevant concentrations. Sterile filter paper discs (10 mm in diameter) were impregnated with the test samples using sterile forceps. The culture bacteria sp. was mixed in saline water, and the turbidity of the bacterial suspension was adjusted to a 0.5 McFarland standard [21]. The bacterial inoculants were spared on Muller Hinton (MH) agar plate. The filter papers that have been impregnated are put on the surface of the MH agar plates. Subsequently, the plates were incubated at a temperature of 37 °C for 24 h, facilitating the proliferation of bacteria and enabling the assessment of the area of inhibition encircling the discs.

To establish the sensitivity of the bacteria to the test samples, we measured the zone of inhibition in millimeters using a ruler. The measurements were then compared to the standard values specified in the CLSI recommendations. The experiment was carried out in triplicate to ensure the results could be reproduced. The positive control for the test was an antibiotic disc containing a recognized antibiotic with action against Pseudomonas sp. and Bacillus sp. The experiment was carried out in triplicate to ensure the results could be reproduced. The positive control for the test was an antibiotic disc containing a recognized antibiotic with action against Pseudomonas sp. and Bacillus sp. The negative control was a blank disc containing only the solvent for dissolving the test samples. The collected data was statistically analyzed to determine the mean and standard deviation.

The PXRD patterns were obtained by conducting measurements on a Rigaku Ultima IV X-ray diffractometer with a scan rate of 10 degrees per minute. The Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) studies were carried out utilizing a Bruker Alpha II ECO-ATR spectrophotometer. The sample was mixed with a KBr pellet, and the measurements were carried out in transmission mode over 32 scans. Electronic transitions were monitored at room temperature by recording UV-Vis spectra utilizing an 1800 SHIMADZU UV-Vis spectrophotometer. The fluorescence spectra were obtained using an F-7000 HITACHI spectrophotometer equipped with a halogen (Xe) lamp as the excitation source. A quartz cell measuring 4 × 1 × 1 cm, equipped with a high vacuum Teﬂon stopcock, was utilized for all fluorescent spectroscopy measurements. The ﬂuorescence lifetime measurements were conducted using Edinburg instruments FSP920, Picosecond Time-resolved cum Steady State Luminescence Spectrometer. The excitation source for the experiment was an LED source with a wavelength of 350 nm. The Zeta potential analysis of the particles was conducted using the Malvern NanoZS90 instrument. This experimental setup utilized a glass cuvette with a square aperture and a zeta dip cell electrode. The experiment was conducted at room temperature. The transmission electron microscope (TEM) data were acquired using a JEOL TEM-2100 model. For TEM analysis, nanocrystalline diamonds (NCDs) were uniformly dispersed onto a 3 mm copper grid coated with a carbon layer. Following this, the samples were stored in a vacuum desiccator.

Determining the fluorescent carbon dots' relative quantum yield (quantum yield, Ф) followed an established procedure [19] . The reference compound utilized in this study was quinine sulfate (Фr = 0.54), which was dissolved in a solution of 0.1 M H2SO4. This solution's refractive index (ηr) was measured to be 1.33. In contrast, the carbon dots were solubilized in deionized (DI) water, with an equivalent refractive index (ηx) of 1.33. The fluorescence spectra of both the reference and carbon dot samples were measured by employing an excitation wavelength of 370 nm. The integrated fluorescence intensity was calculated by measuring the area under the fluorescence curve within the 380–700 nm wavelength range. Subsequently, the reference and sample slopes were determined by plotting the obtained data. The quantum yield was obtained by employing Eq. (1),

Where Ф represents the quantum yield, η signifies the solvent's refractive index, Grad stands for the slope derived from the graph plotted between absorbance and integrated fluorescence intensity, and x and r stand for sample and reference, respectively [70].

The synthesis scheme for N-doped carbon dots (NCDs) is depicted in Scheme 1. Upon excitation with a wavelength of 365 nm, the purified NCDs exhibited a distinct blue fluorescence. Scheme 1 also illustrates the environmental sensing applications of C-dots.

The UV–visible and fluorescence spectroscopy techniques were employed to analyze the optical properties of NCDs in deionized water. This analysis is illustrated in Fig. 1a and 1b. The carbon dots' specific absorption and emission properties in aqueous solutions were examined through UV–vis spectroscopy and photoluminescence spectrophotometry (PL). The UV–vis spectrum displayed two absorption bands at 270 and 325 nm (Fig. 1a). The aromatic sp2 ππ* transitions can potentially explain the absorption peak observed at a wavelength of 270 nm, between the CC and C-C bonds (graphitic cores) of NCDs.

Additionally, the absorption band at 325 nm consists of the n to π * transitions associated with CO groups on the surface of NCDs. Both of the absorption bands are characteristic of CQDs. The photoluminescence spectra of the NCDs under excitation of 300–400 nm showed the optimal excitation wavelength (λex) to be at 370 nm (as shown in Fig. 1b). This excitation wavelength resulted in a blue fluorescent emission at an emission wavelength (λem) of 456 nm. Significantly, the emission behaviour shifted towards longer wavelengths in the maximum emission wavelength and increased the intensity of photoluminescence (PL) when the excitation wavelength ranged from 300 to 370 nm. In contrast, it was observed that excitations with lower energy levels (370–400 nm) resulted in a subsequent red-shift in the emission wavelength and a consistent reduction in the photoluminescence (PL) intensity. This phenomenon is commonly observed in carbon dots exhibiting excitation-dependent emission behavior [15,37].

The observed red-shift phenomenon can be ascribed to the increased transitions within the graphitic core of individual sp 2 clusters, which occur at lower excitation energies [18]. The NCDs demonstrated a quantum yield value of 21%, which was ascertained through calibration utilizing quinine sulfate (in 0.1 M H2SO4) as a reference point. The quantum yield value reported in this study is consistent with previous research, showing that synthesized NCDs typically exhibit high quantum yields [36,41,63]. This outcome indicates that we can modify the quantum yield of our NCDs further so that they can be effectively employed in various applications that require highly luminescent materials.

The surface functionalities of NCDs were examined using Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy, as illustrated in Fig. 1c. The presence of solid peaks at wavenumbers 3428 cm−1 and 3381 cm−1 provides evidence for the occurrence of asymmetric stretching vibrations in the –OH and N–H functional groups, respectively [61,63,66]. The vibrational band at 1634 cm−1 corresponds to the amide linkage (OC–NH), which is consistent with prior reports [45]. The absorption bands observed at wavenumbers 1327 cm−1 and 2951 cm−1 are assigned to the stretching vibrations of C−O and C−H bonds, respectively. On the other hand, the absorption band at 1572 cm−1 is attributed to the bending vibrations of the N − H bond. The bands observed at 1162 cm−1 and 1482 cm−1 are assigned as C—NH—C and C-N stretching vibrations, respectively [62,72].

A study by Xu et al. identified bands ranging from approximately 400 to 800 cm−1 in the extensive spectrum of C-dots, attributing them to the presence of flavonoids [66]. In the FTIR spectrum of NCDs, we observed a similar pattern, characterized by a peak at 812 cm−1, suggesting the presence of flavonoids. Considering our results in conjunction with existing literature, we can deduce that our NCDs contain various functional groups, including amine, hydroxyl, amide, and carbonyl moieties.

Fig. 1d depicts the PXRD pattern of synthesized NCDs. The broad hump at 2θ value of 23.2° can be indexed as the (002) plane of hexagonal graphitic structure [3].

Here, we employed Bragg's Eq. (2) to determine the d-spacing of NCDs, considering the parameters: n, a positive integer (n = 1); λ (1.54 Å) is the wavelength of the incident X-ray; and θ, representing the position of the (002) peak. The d-spacing value for the (002) peak of NCDs was approximately 0.38 nm, consistent with the earlier reports of CDs [73]. Notably, the broadening of the XRD peaks of NCDs suggested the presence of small-sized carbon dots. The synthesized NCDs' morphology was characterized using a Transmission electron microscope (TEM) ( Fig. 2a). Fig. 2b illustrates the average size distribution of NCDs resulting from the TEM image of the 20 nm scale. The NCDs are approximately spherical and have an average diameter of 4.83 nm according to the distribution curve ranging from 2.5 to 7.0 nm. The results of TEM measurements are also confirmed by Dynamic Light Scattering (DLS) analysis, as shown in Fig. 2c.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence-mediated sensing of Cr6+ and Mn7+
ce:section.ce:para: Considering the inherent fluorescent properties of carbon dots and their pH stability within the range of pH 3 to 10, it was deemed essential to assess their sensitivity towards various ions. Further investigations revealed that the synthesized NCDs exhibited sensitivity toward both Chromium (VI) (Cr6+) and Manganese (VII) (Mn7+) ions. Cr6+ ion is a mutagenic, carcinogenic, and highly toxic environmental hazard that enters water resources through anthropogenic activities [49,75]. Additionally, Mn7+ ion is known for its toxicity and ability to cause respiratory damage when inhaled [27]. Here, we have employed NCDs to detect the presence of these two pollutants in water. However, the selectivity of the NCDs towards them was particularly significant compared to a broad spectrum of other environmentally relevant cations. These included metal cations like, Ag+, Zn2+, Cd2+, Ca2+, Pb2+, Hg2+, Cu2+, Fe3+, Mg2+, Mn2+ and Ni2+, as well as the anions Cl–, NO3 –, F–, I–, CO3 2–, HPO4 –, HPO4 2– and S2–. All the ions were tested in an aqueous solution at a concentration of 150 µM ( Fig. 3a,b,c,d). Experimental procedures were replicated thrice for each ion to ensure precision and reliability, while special focus was imparted on Cr6+ and Mn7+ during the experimental procedure.

The sensitivity exhibited by the fluorescent probe (NCDs) towards Cr6+ and Mn7+ was subjected to extensive examination to ascertain their respective detection limit (LOD) upon the introduction of Cr6+ ( Fig. 4a, b) or Mn7+ (Fig. 4d, e), the fluorescence exhibited by NCD at a wavelength of 456 nm experienced a quenching effect. A corresponding reduction in emission intensity was noted, displaying a linear relationship with increasing concentrations of Cr6+ (Fig. 4c) or Mn7+ species (Fig. 4f). Notably, no discernible shift was observed in the peak position of the fluorescence emission maximum. Calibration graphs were plotted utilizing the emission spectra in the presence of both analytes by aligning the Fo–F (fluorescence quenching efficiency) against the various analyte concentrations. The calculation for the corresponding.

detection limit was facilitated by applying the equation LOD = 3.3 × RSD/Slope. The calculated detection limits for Cr6+ and Mn7+ were 0.173 µM and 0.394 µM, respectively. These values underscore the high sensitivity of the prepared sensor, as depicted in Fig. 4.

NCDs' stability and their fluorescence properties under varying pH conditions were investigated. This is of critical importance as changes in pH can significantly affect a sensor's sensitivity and selectivity towards metal ions in environmental or biological matrices. In the pursuit of determining the ideal conditions for detecting the fluorescence turn-off of Cr6+ and Mn7+, we measured fluorescence intensities while introducing 100 µM concentrations of Cr6+ and Mn7+ into a PBS (Phosphate-Buffered Saline) buffer solution. The pH of the solution was varied across a range from 1.0 to 11.0, and the respective fluorescence intensity was recorded thoroughly ( Fig. 5).

The fluorescence intensity of the NCDs demonstrated minimal alteration over a wide pH span (pH 3–7). Nonetheless, a notable emissive intensity change was observed under intensely acidic (pH 1–3) and primary (pH 9–11) conditions. This reduction in intensity is likely attributable to the disruption of oxygen functionalities randomly dispersed across the NCD's surface, thereby adversely affecting their inherent fluorescent behavior. Furthermore, the NCD emissions within the severely acidic and basic solutions detected a minor yet considerable red shift. This occurrence may be associated with diminished emissivity from NCDs' surface, facilitating more distinct emissions from the graphitic center than surface emissions [5]. The observed changes in spectral properties can be utilized to evaluate the ideal conditions that impact the fluorescence response of the NCD-based sensor at different pH levels.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of parameters for effective ﬂuorescence quenching
ce:section.ce:para: The ideal parameters for achieving maximum fluorescence quenching efficiency have been comprehensively delineated in the Supplementary Files, specifically referenced within Fig. SF1 to SF2.

The phenomenon of fluorescence quenching is regulated by various mechanistic pathways, such as Forster resonance energy transfer (FRET), static quenching, photoinduced electron transfer (PET), dynamic quenching, the inner filter effect (IFE), and aggregation-caused quenching (ACQ). In our investigation to elucidate the plausible mechanistic pathways, we evaluated the potential intersection between the acceptor's absorption and the donor's emission spectra, depicted in Fig. 6. Cr6+ and Mn7+ exhibit distinct absorption spectra, which may significantly overlap with the fluorescence emission spectra of NCDs at 456 nm. This finding strongly suggests that Fluorescence Resonance Energy Transfer (FRET) or Inner Filter Effect (IFE) could be responsible for the observed quenching phenomena. Both these mechanisms depend upon a critical condition that the fluorophore's emission spectrum should overlap significantly with the absorption spectrum of analytes, which appears to be satisfied in this case. Therefore, it is plausible that the interactions between NCDs and Cr6+ or Mn7+ could be mediated via FRET or IFE, contributing to the observed fluorescence quenching [1,64] . To verify the energy transition from NCDs to Cr6+ or Mn7+, time-resolved photoluminescence (TRPL) measurements are performed. This approach is chosen because decay time measurements exhibit greater sensitivity and reliable indicators than fluorescence quenching efficiencies. Fig. 7 depicts the TRPL spectra of NCDs in the presence of the analytes. Analyzing Fig. 7(a, b), it is evident that a significant reduction in decay time is noted for NCD upon introducing Cr6+ or Mn7+. Initially, NCDs exhibited a lifetime of 3.54 ns, adding Cr6+ and Mn7+ changes to 2.52 ns and 2.49 ns, respectively. The observed decrease in the average lifespan can be attributed to the quenching interactions occurring within the Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) mechanism [34]. In the case of IFE, the unaffected fluorescence lifetime of the probe before and after adding the analyte is the primary indication of the involved mechanism [13].

In general, quenching mechanisms can be classified into two primary categories, static and dynamic. Static quenching involves forming a non-radiative complex between the fluorophore and the analyte while they are in their respective ground states. In this mechanism, even with increasing analyte concentration, the intrinsic lifetime of the sensor system remains unaffected. Dynamic quenching is characterized by electron transfer due to collisions between the excited fluorophore and the quencher molecule. This collisional phenomenon reduces the fluorophore's average lifetime as quencher concentration increases. Thus, the considerable reduction in fluorescence lifetime observed for NCDs after adding Cr6+ or Mn7+ also validated dynamic quenching in the detection method [57].

However, this does not rule out the potential occurrence of static quenching. Fig. 8(a, b) and 8(c,d) display the Stern-Volmer plots for Cr6+ and Mn7+, respectively. The process based on FRET exhibited a consistent linear correlation between the fluorescence intensity and the increasing concentration of analytes within the lower range. These graphs illustrate the correlation between the fluorescence quenching capacity and the lower concentration of these analytes (Fig. 8a, b), based on the Eq. (3),
(3) Fo/F = 1 + Ksv·C

In the given context, F and Fo stand for fluorescence intensity of NCDs in aqueous media, with and without the presence of the analyte, respectively. The symbol 'C' refers to the concentration of the analyte. The expression for linear regression, resulting from the Stern Volmer (S-V) plot, (Fo/F) - 1 = 0.034 C (μM) – 0.20, gives R2 = 0.97 for Cr6+ and (Fo/F) - 1 = 0.016 C (μM) – 0.082 gives R2 = 0.99 for Mn7+. For lower concentrations of analyte, the Stern Volmer plot's linearity supports the static nature of quenching. While increasing the analyte concentration, non-linear deviation appears in the S-V plot corresponding to dynamic quenching behavior (Fig. 8c, 8d). The S-V plot implied the simultaneous operation of static and dynamic quenching mechanisms in our given sensing system [43,53]. This situation occurs when both processes compete and the reactants are closely aligned, with minimal influence from diffusion [33].

Furthermore, the selectivity in Cr6+ and Mn7+ sensing can be elucidated by the electrostatic interactions between fluorophores (NCDs) and the quencher [6,16]. To validate this, we have acquired quantitative absorbance spectra for NCDs after the addition of rising concentrations of Cr6+ and Mn7+, as illustrated in Fig. 9(a) and 9(b), respectively. This observation confirms the emergence of new absorption bands at 368 and 269 nm in the UV-Vis spectrum of NCDs upon adding Cr6+. In the case of Mn7+, the absorption band of bare Mn7+ near 520 nm disappeared after addition with NCDs. This might be due to the redox transition of Mn7+ (MnO4 -) to Mn4+ (MnO2), while it is complexed with NCDs due to photoinduced oxidative electron transfer (PET) reaction [35]. This result is confirmed by the visual colour change experiment and the corresponding PXRD data (Fig. SF3). However, the TRPL plot (Fig. 7b) and the non-linear S-V plot of NCDs in the presence of Mn7+ (Fig. 8d) signifies that the FRET mechanism is operated by the significant fluorescence quenching of the probe, in contrast to PET. This is because the effectiveness of FRET for fluorescence quenching is considerably greater than that of PET [60,64].

To further study the potential interaction during the quenching process involving NCDs and Cr6+/Mn7+, we have experimented with measuring the Zeta potential before and after adding analyte species. Fig. SF4 highlights the baseline Zeta potential values for NCDs (−6.33 mV), NCDs +Cr6+ (−25.1 mV), and NCDs +Mn7+ (−34.0 mV). The noticeable shift in the values of the zeta potential of NCDs after adding analytes suggested a probable electrostatic interaction between NCDs and analytes. The observed significant increase in the zeta potential values of NCDs+Cr6+ and NCDs +Mn7+ can be attributed to the surface adsorption mechanism, wherein the negatively charged Cr2O7 2− and MnO4 − ions interact electrostatically with the surface of NCDs [24].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Investigation of the practical utility of the sensor
ce:section.ce:para: Furthermore, we have explored the practical utility of NCDs in real-sample analysis by introducing Cr6+ and Mn7+ to tap water and soil-extracted samples. With the incremental introduction of these analytes, there was a noticeable decline in the emission peak, as depicted in Fig. 10(a,c,e,g). Relevant calibration plots are also illustrated in Fig. 10(b,d,f,h).

Detection limits were ascertained using the standard calibration curve approach, resulting in 0.48 and 0.39 µM for Cr6+ and 0.59 and 0.30 µM for Mn7+ in tap water and soil samples, respectively. These findings underscore the nano-probe's reliability and precision, highlighting its ability to determine contamination levels in soil samples and industrial wastewater.

Studies on sensing Cr6+ and Mn7+ in tap water (TW) and soil samples (SS), along with the recoveries at five different concentrations of the analytes, are summarized in Table SF1 and SF2. The efficacy of this technique was assessed by determining the spike recovery, which showed a range of 99.21–101.5% and 99.15–101.7% for Cr6+ in TW and SS, respectively, while for Mn7+ in TW and SS showed 98.9–100.4% and 99.1–100.3% respectively. The corresponding %RSD value suggested the sensor's reliability and precision in determining Cr6+ and Mn7+ in real samples [2,22].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Visual detection of Cr6+ and Mn7+
ce:section.ce:para: For the visual identification of Cr6+ and Mn7+, we captured digital images of NCD solutions with varying concentrations of Cr6+ and Mn7+ illuminated by 365 nm UV light. As depicted in Fig. 11(a, b), the bright blue fluorescence emission of NCDs gradually diminished with an increase in the concentration of analytes in an aqueous medium. In an alternative approach, we coated the pieces of filter paper with NCDs and observed the alterations in their fluorescence response with various concentrations of Cr6+ and Mn7+ when exposed to UV light, as depicted in Fig. (c, d). The outcomes are very close to those obtained in the solution phase. Leveraging filter paper represents a remarkably cost-efficient strategy for detecting diverse pollutants. Paper-based sensors are uncomplicated and can be conveniently kept under regular environmental conditions [28]. Consequently, this technique is highly efficient for producing Cr6+ and Mn7+ sensors at minimal expense by treating filter papers with NCDs.

The Kirby-Bauer Disc Diffusion Susceptibility Test Protocol was employed to investigate the antibacterial efficacy of the test samples against Pseudomonas sp. and Bacillus sp. The test results indicate that there was no observable zone of inhibition against any of the bacterial strains that were subjected to analysis. A zone of inhibition refers to the region surrounding a disc where bacterial growth is not observed, indicating the presence of antibacterial activity in the tested sample [25]. However, in this study, no zone of inhibition was observed, which suggests that the test samples did not possess notable antibacterial properties against Pseudomonas sp. and Bacillus sp. within the tested conditions (Figure SF4). The lack of antibacterial properties can be highlighted as an advantageous feature of NCDs that can be practically used as non-hazardous sensors.

Various techniques are available in the literature for detecting Cr6 + and Mn7 + ions in aqueous media. The fabricated NCDs are compared with the previously reported fluorescence-based sensors for detecting Cr6+ and Mn7+ ions in Table SF3. However, compared to the previously reported methods, the following are some key features of our developed nano-sensor:
(i)
Cost-effective- The present work reports the synthesis of highly luminescent N-doped carbon dots using a green source, i.e., Alstonia scholaris leaves and ethylenediamine as precursors. The prepared NCDs are non-hazardous, cost-effective, and synthesized using a green route relative to the reported fluorescence-driven sensors.
(ii)
Appreciable detection limit- The probe acts as a dual-sensing platform and efficiently detects both Cr6+ and Mn7+. The developed material has been utilized as a fluorescence turn-off and filter paper-based sensing platform. The synthesized NCDs have a notable detection limit of about 0.173 µM and 0.394 µM for Cr6+ and Mn7+, respectively.
(iii)
Effective towards real sample analysis- The photophysical techniques are viable for real samples like tap water and soil-extracted samples with a considerable detection limit for both Cr6+ and Mn7+ ions.

Fluorescent carbon dots derived from natural precursors are considered an emerging material for the facile and efficient sensing hazardous heavy metal ions in aqueous media. A novel and green pathway has been adopted for synthesizing nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs) from Alstonia scholaris leaves and ethylenediamine using a simple one-step hydrothermal approach. The prepared NCDs possess an efficient quantum yield of about 21%. Different analytical techniques such as UV–vis spectroscopy, FT-IR spectroscopy, XRD, and TEM analysis have been carried out to characterize the developed material. NCDs were then utilized as a fluorescent sensing platform for detecting Cr6+ and Mn7+ in aqueous media via dual detection mode.

Along with fluorescence turn-off, we have also used it as a filter paper-based sensing platform. The NCDs possess appreciable LODs of about 0.173 µM and 0.394 µM for sensing Cr6+ and Mn7+, respectively. The fluorescence turn-off involves the FRET mechanism, validated through various experimental findings. Through analysis of real samples like tap water and soil-extracted samples, we have determined the practical usability of our developed sensing platform. Additionally, we have also fabricated a filter paper-based sensing platform for the detection of Cr6+ and Mn7+. These filter paper-based detection techniques are beneficial and easy for rapidly detecting toxic metal ions in water. Concisely, we have synthesized novel NCDs using green and cost-effective precursors and utilized them as a nano-sensor for efficiently sensing Cr6+ and Mn7+ ions in aqueous media and real samples with significant LODs.

Rachita Newar: Developed the concept of Cr6+ and Mn7+ detection using fluorescent measurements, preparation of samples, sensing experiments, data analysis, and manuscript preparation. Nasrin Sultana: Characterization of synthesized material using TEM and TRPL. Saikat Das synthesized the materials in bulk quantities. Bhoirob Gogoic, Nazim Forid: Analysed the Antibacterial property of synthesized material, Hemen Sarma: Technical support, writing, review and editing the original manuscript. Arabinda Baruah: Overviewed the entire project and Foundation.
